title: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
summary: User-supplied input was observed reflected in the HTTP response without context-appropriate encoding. An attacker may craft a URL containing malicious JavaScript that, when clicked by a victim, executes in the victim's browser session with full access to the page context, cookies, and session data.
why_it_matters: Reflected XSS is one of the most prevalent web vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10 A03:2021 Injection). A successful exploit allows an attacker to steal session tokens, impersonate users, redirect to phishing pages, deface content, or install keyloggers — all by tricking a user into clicking a crafted link. XSS attacks bypass same-origin policy protections because the malicious script executes within the trusted origin. Automated scanners and attackers routinely probe for reflection points, making unencoded output a high-priority finding.
how_to_fix:
Apply context-aware output encoding to all user-controlled data before including it in HTTP responses:

1. **HTML context** — HTML-entity encode user input before inserting it into HTML body content. Encode `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, and `'` at minimum.
2. **HTML attribute context** — Attribute-encode user input and always quote attribute values. Never insert user input into unquoted attributes or event handler attributes.
3. **JavaScript context** — JavaScript-encode (hex escape) user input before inserting it into script blocks. Prefer assigning values via DOM APIs rather than inline interpolation.
4. **URL context** — URL-encode user input before inserting it into URL parameters. Validate that URLs begin with `https://` or a relative path (never `javascript:` or `data:`).
5. **CSS context** — CSS-encode user input before inserting it into style blocks or attributes.
6. **Use framework auto-escaping** — Modern frameworks (React, Angular, Vue, Django templates, Thymeleaf, Razor) auto-escape by default. Never bypass these protections (e.g., `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, `|safe`, `{!! !!}`) without thorough review.
7. **Deploy Content-Security-Policy** — A strict CSP provides defense-in-depth by preventing execution of injected scripts even if an encoding gap exists.
unsafe_example:
Search results for: ${userInput}

// UNSAFE: server-side rendering without encoding (Node.js/Express) app.get('/search', (req, res) => { const query = req.query.q; res.send(`

You searched for: ${query}

`); });
developer_notes: Encoding must be context-specific. HTML-entity encoding is not sufficient for JavaScript or URL contexts. The most common XSS mistakes are: (1) using raw string interpolation in server-rendered templates, (2) bypassing framework auto-escaping with raw/safe directives, and (3) using innerHTML or document.write with user input on the client side. For React applications, avoid `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` unless the content has been sanitized with a library like DOMPurify. For server-rendered applications (Express, Django, Spring MVC), ensure the templating engine's auto-escaping is enabled globally and not overridden on a per-template basis.
verification_checklist: All user-controlled input is encoded using context-appropriate encoding (HTML, attribute, JavaScript, URL) before being included in responses
Framework auto-escaping is enabled globally and no templates use raw/safe/unescaped output directives without documented justification
Client-side code does not use innerHTML, document.write, or outerHTML with user-controlled data
Content-Security-Policy header is deployed with a strict script-src directive as defense-in-depth
Automated tests submit XSS probe payloads and verify they are rendered as text, not executed

references.title: CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (XSS)
OWASP DOM-based XSS Prevention Cheat Sheet
